title: The Wealth of Nations
subtitle: An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations
author: Adam Smith
description: The foundational text of classical economics. Adam Smith's 1776 masterpiece investigates the forces that create national prosperity — the division of labor, the function of markets, the role of self-interest, and the proper scope of government. It introduced the invisible hand, the labor theory of value, and the theory of absolute advantage. No single work has done more to shape modern economic thought.
keyIdeas: The division of labor is the primary engine of productivity growth
Self-interest, not benevolence, drives economic exchange
The invisible hand coordinates individual pursuits into social benefit
Labor is the real measure of exchangeable value
Productive labor creates tangible goods; unproductive labor does not
Free trade according to absolute advantage benefits all nations
Land, labor, and capital are the three factors of production
Government should be limited to defense, justice, and public works
Markets tend toward equilibrium through price signals
The natural price is the long-run competitive price covering costs plus normal profit
keyTakeaways: The division of labor is limited by the extent of the market — larger markets enable greater specialization
People act in their own interest, and this self-interest, when channeled through competitive markets, produces socially beneficial outcomes
The value of a good has two meanings: value in use (utility) and value in exchange (purchasing power)
Labor is the real price of everything — what a thing costs is the labor required to acquire it
Money is not wealth; real wealth consists of the goods and services a nation can produce
Free trade allows each country to specialize in what it produces most efficiently, benefiting all trading partners
The sovereign has only three duties: defense, justice, and certain public works that are unprofitable for private enterprise
Monopolies and mercantilist restrictions enrich the few at the expense of the many
Wages tend to rise in growing economies and stagnate in stationary ones
The accumulation of capital is the precondition for sustained growth in the division of labor
whoShouldRead: Anyone who wants to understand the intellectual foundations of capitalism
Students of economics, political science, history, and philosophy
Business leaders and entrepreneurs seeking to understand market dynamics
Policymakers and public intellectuals debating the role of government in the economy
Readers of modern economics who want to see where the core ideas originated
